glossary.definition: The phospholipid bilayer that encloses all cells, regulates transport, and mediates cell signaling.
Membrane-bound organelle in eukaryotes that houses the cell's DNA and serves as the site of transcription.
Double-membrane organelles that are the primary site of aerobic ATP production via oxidative phosphorylation.
Network of membranes involved in protein synthesis (rough ER) and lipid synthesis and detoxification (smooth ER).
Organelle that receives, modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids for secretion or delivery to other organelles.
Organelle containing hydrolytic enzymes that degrade macromolecules and cellular debris at low pH.
Ribonucleoprotein complex (large and small subunits) that catalyzes translation of mRNA into protein.
Network of microfilaments (actin), intermediate filaments, and microtubules that maintains cell shape and enables movement.
Passive diffusion of water across a semipermeable membrane from a region of lower solute concentration to higher solute concentration.
Movement of molecules against their concentration gradient, requiring energy (ATP) and specific carrier proteins.
Ordered sequence of events (G1, S, G2, M) by which a cell grows and divides into two daughter cells.
Division of the cytoplasm following nuclear division, producing two separate daughter cells.
Cell-cell junction that creates an impermeable seal between epithelial cells, preventing paracellular transport.
The fundamental unit of life; a membrane-bound compartment containing genetic material and metabolic machinery. Prokaryotes lack a nucleus; eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus.
A phospholipid bilayer (with embedded proteins) that bounds cells and organelles; regulates transport of materials, maintains electrochemical gradients, and mediates signaling.
A living entity capable of carrying out metabolism, growth, reproduction, and response to stimuli; may be unicellular (bacteria, archaea, protists) or multicellular.

canon.name: Fluid mosaic model
canon.form: The plasma membrane is a dynamic, selectively permeable phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins that can move laterally.

canon.name: Mitosis stages
canon.form: Prophase → Metaphase → Anaphase → Telophase → Cytokinesis; produces two genetically identical diploid daughter cells.

canon.name: Meiosis overview
canon.form: Two sequential divisions (Meiosis I: homolog separation; Meiosis II: sister chromatid separation) producing four genetically unique haploid gametes.

canon.name: Endosymbiotic theory
canon.form: Mitochondria and chloroplasts arose from ancestral prokaryotes engulfed by a host cell; evidenced by double membranes, circular DNA, and 70S ribosomes.

canon.name: Vesicular trafficking
canon.form: Proteins synthesized on rough ER are packaged into vesicles, modified in the Golgi (cis → medial → trans), and dispatched to lysosomes, plasma membrane, or extracellular space.

canon.name: Passive vs. active transport
canon.form: Passive (diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis): down concentration gradient, no ATP. Active: against gradient, requires ATP (primary) or gradient (secondary).

canon.name: Signal transduction cascade
canon.form: Extracellular ligand binds receptor → intracellular second messengers (cAMP, Ca²⁺, IP3) → kinase cascades → gene expression or metabolic changes.

subtopics: Oxidative phosphorylation and electron transport chain

glossary.definition: A biological catalyst (usually protein) that accelerates chemical reactions by lowering activation energy without being consumed.
The specific molecule upon which an enzyme acts, binding at the active site.
The substrate concentration at which reaction velocity is half of Vmax; reflects enzyme-substrate affinity (lower Km = higher affinity).
The maximum reaction velocity achieved when all enzyme active sites are saturated with substrate.
Inhibitor resembles substrate and binds reversibly to the active site; raises apparent Km, Vmax unchanged; overcome by increasing substrate.
Regulation of enzyme activity by a molecule binding to a site other than the active site, causing conformational change.
Adenosine triphosphate; the primary cellular energy currency, releasing energy upon hydrolysis of its terminal phosphate bond.
Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide; electron carrier that accepts (NAD+ → NADH) or donates electrons (NADH → NAD+) in metabolic reactions.
The ten-step cytoplasmic pathway that converts one glucose into two pyruvate, yielding a net 2 ATP and 2 NADH.
The mitochondrial process coupling electron transfer through the ETC to ATP synthesis via ATP synthase, driven by the proton gradient.
The mitochondrial process that sequentially cleaves two-carbon acetyl-CoA units from fatty acids, generating NADH, FADH2, and acetyl-CoA.
Synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors (pyruvate, lactate, glycerol, amino acids), primarily in the liver.
Regulation in which the end product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an earlier enzyme in the same pathway.
The linear sequence of amino acid residues in a polypeptide (or of nucleotides in a nucleic acid), encoded by the gene and determining all higher-order folding.
Local, regular folding of a polypeptide backbone into alpha-helices and beta-sheets, stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone atoms.
The overall three-dimensional conformation of a single folded polypeptide, stabilized by side-chain interactions: hydrophobic packing, disulfide bonds, salt bridges, and hydrogen bonds.

canon.name: Michaelis-Menten equation
canon.form: v = Vmax[S] / (Km + [S])

canon.name: Glycolysis net reaction
canon.form: Glucose + 2 NAD⁺ + 2 ADP + 2 Pi → 2 Pyruvate + 2 NADH + 2 ATP + 2 H₂O

canon.name: Citric acid cycle (TCA cycle)
canon.form: Acetyl-CoA enters, condenses with oxaloacetate → citrate; 8 steps yield 3 NADH, 1 FADH2, 1 GTP per turn; CO2 released; oxaloacetate regenerated.

canon.name: Electron transport chain
canon.form: NADH and FADH2 donate electrons to Complexes I–IV; electrons ultimately reduce O2 to H2O; proton gradient drives ATP synthase (Complex V); ~34 ATP per glucose.

canon.name: Glucose catabolism ATP yield
canon.form: Complete oxidation of glucose: ~30–32 ATP (2 glycolysis + 2 TCA substrate-level + ~26–28 oxidative phosphorylation).

canon.name: Competitive vs. non-competitive inhibition
canon.form: Competitive: Km↑, Vmax unchanged. Non-competitive: Km unchanged, Vmax↓. Uncompetitive: both Km and Vmax↓.

canon.name: Metabolic integration
canon.form: Fed state: insulin promotes glycolysis, glycogenesis, lipogenesis. Fasting: glucagon/epinephrine promote glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis, lipolysis, beta-oxidation.

glossary.definition: Semiconservative copying of DNA by DNA polymerase III (prokaryotes) / Pol δ, ε (eukaryotes), always 5'→3', requiring an RNA primer.
Synthesis of an RNA molecule complementary to the DNA template strand, catalyzed by RNA polymerase.
Ribosomal synthesis of a polypeptide chain from an mRNA template, reading codons 5'→3' and assembling amino acids N→C.
A three-nucleotide sequence in mRNA that specifies one amino acid or a stop signal; the genetic code is read in non-overlapping triplets.
DNA sequence upstream of a gene to which RNA polymerase and transcription factors bind to initiate transcription.
A unit of prokaryotic gene regulation consisting of a promoter, operator, and co-transcribed structural genes (e.g., the lac operon).
Post-transcriptional removal of introns and ligation of exons by the spliceosome to produce mature mRNA in eukaryotes.
AUG; codes for methionine (Met) and defines the start of the open reading frame in translation.
UAA, UAG, or UGA; codons that signal termination of translation and release of the polypeptide.
Enzyme that synthesizes new DNA strands in the 5'→3' direction using a template; requires a primer and has 3'→5' proofreading exonuclease activity.
Endonuclease that cleaves double-stranded DNA at specific recognition sequences; used as a tool in recombinant DNA technology.
Polymerase chain reaction; in vitro method to exponentially amplify a specific DNA region using primers, Taq polymerase, and thermal cycling.
Heritable changes in gene expression that do not involve changes in DNA sequence, mediated by histone modification, DNA methylation, and chromatin remodeling.
Deoxyribonucleic acid; a double-stranded helix of nucleotides (A, T, G, C) encoding genetic information; the hereditary molecule replicated and transcribed in all cellular life.
Ribonucleic acid; a single-stranded polymer of nucleotides (A, U, G, C) that carries genetic information (mRNA), forms ribosome components (rRNA), and transfers amino acids (tRNA).
A segment of DNA encoding a functional product (protein or RNA); the basic unit of heredity transmitted from parent to offspring; genes are organized on chromosomes.
A polymer of amino acids (polypeptide) that folds into a specific 3D structure to perform structural, catalytic, signaling, or transport functions; encoded by genes.
A long DNA molecule associated with proteins (histones in eukaryotes) that packages the genome; humans have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs) in diploid cells.

canon.name: Central dogma
canon.form: DNA → (transcription) → RNA → (translation) → Protein; information flows from nucleic acid sequence to amino acid sequence.

canon.name: Semiconservative replication
canon.form: Each new DNA double helix contains one original (template) strand and one newly synthesized strand; demonstrated by Meselson-Stahl experiment.

canon.name: Lac operon regulation
canon.form: In absence of lactose: repressor binds operator, blocking transcription. In presence of lactose: allolactose binds repressor, releases operator, allows transcription. cAMP-CAP provides positive regulation when glucose is absent.

canon.name: Eukaryotic transcription regulation
canon.form: Transcription factors bind enhancers/silencers; mediator complex bridges to RNA Pol II at the promoter; chromatin accessibility (histone acetylation) gates access.

canon.name: mRNA processing in eukaryotes
canon.form: 5' 7-methylguanosine cap addition → splicing of introns by spliceosome → 3' poly-A tail addition; produces mature mRNA exported to cytoplasm.

canon.name: Genetic code properties
canon.form: Triplet codons (64 total: 61 sense + 3 stop); degenerate (multiple codons per amino acid); universal across nearly all life; non-overlapping and comma-free.

canon.name: DNA repair mechanisms
canon.form: Mismatch repair (corrects replication errors); nucleotide excision repair (bulky lesions, UV damage); base excision repair (modified bases); homologous recombination / NHEJ (double-strand breaks).

glossary.definition: One of two or more alternative forms of a gene at a given locus on a chromosome.
The genetic constitution of an organism at one or more loci.
The observable physical or biochemical characteristics of an organism, resulting from genotype and environment.
An allele whose phenotypic effect is expressed in heterozygotes (one copy sufficient); conventionally denoted by uppercase.
An allele whose phenotypic effect is expressed only in homozygotes; masked by the dominant allele in heterozygotes.
Having two identical alleles at a given locus (AA or aa).
Having two different alleles at a given locus (Aa).
Cross between an individual of unknown genotype and a homozygous recessive individual to determine the unknown genotype from offspring ratios.
Both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote (e.g., ABO blood type IA and IB alleles).
Heterozygote shows an intermediate phenotype between the two homozygotes (e.g., red × white → pink flowers).
Interaction between genes at different loci in which one gene modifies or masks the expression of another.
Tendency of genes located close together on the same chromosome to be inherited together, deviating from independent assortment.
The proportion of recombinant offspring from a cross; used as a measure of genetic distance (1% recombination = 1 centimorgan).
A group of organisms that can interbreed and produce fertile offspring under natural conditions; defined by reproductive isolation (biological species concept) or phylogenetic criteria.
A heritable change in the DNA sequence; types include point mutations, insertions, deletions, chromosomal rearrangements. Mutations are the raw material for evolution.
The phenotype or allele of an organism as it predominantly occurs in nature, used as the reference standard against which mutants are compared.

canon.name: Mendel's Law of Segregation
canon.form: Each diploid organism carries two alleles for each trait, which separate during gamete formation so each gamete carries only one allele.

canon.name: Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment
canon.form: Alleles of different genes assort independently into gametes, provided the genes are on different chromosomes (not linked).

canon.name: Monohybrid cross ratios
canon.form: Aa × Aa → 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa (3:1 phenotypic ratio for dominant trait).

canon.name: Dihybrid cross ratio
canon.form: AaBb × AaBb → 9 A_B_ : 3 A_bb : 3 aaB_ : 1 aabb (9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio).

canon.name: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium
canon.form: p² + 2pq + q² = 1; p + q = 1; allele frequencies stay constant in a large, randomly mating, non-evolving population.

canon.name: Sex-linked inheritance
canon.form: X-linked recessive traits expressed in hemizygous males (XY); females are carriers (X^A X^a); never passed father-to-son.

canon.name: Chi-square test for genetics
canon.form: χ² = Σ(observed − expected)² / expected; used to test whether observed ratios deviate from Mendelian expectations.

glossary.definition: Differential survival and reproduction of individuals with heritable traits that increase fitness in a given environment.
An organism's reproductive success relative to others; the measure of contribution to the gene pool of the next generation.
A heritable trait that increases an organism's fitness in its environment, shaped by natural selection over generations.
Random changes in allele frequencies in a population, most pronounced in small populations; includes bottleneck and founder effects.
Transfer of alleles between populations through migration of individuals or gametes; tends to homogenize allele frequencies.
The evolutionary process by which populations diverge to form reproductively isolated species; allopatric (geographic) and sympatric are the main modes.
Barriers (prezygotic or postzygotic) that prevent interbreeding between populations; the defining criterion of biological species.
Speciation driven by geographic isolation of populations, allowing independent divergence through selection and drift.
A branching diagram (phylogenetic tree or cladogram) depicting evolutionary relationships among taxa based on shared derived characters.
Structural or molecular similarity between species due to shared ancestry (e.g., vertebrate forelimbs); distinguished from analogy (convergent evolution).
The concept that DNA and protein sequences accumulate mutations at an approximately constant rate, allowing estimation of divergence times.
Selection that favors one extreme phenotype, shifting the population mean in one direction.

canon.name: Darwinian natural selection prerequisites
canon.form: Four conditions required: (1) variation in traits, (2) traits are heritable, (3) differential survival/reproduction, (4) environment is selective.

canon.name: Types of natural selection
canon.form: Directional (favors one extreme) → shifts mean; Stabilizing (favors intermediate) → reduces variance; Disruptive (favors both extremes) → bimodal distribution.

canon.name: Hardy-Weinberg conditions (evolution null model)
canon.form: No evolution if: large population, random mating, no mutation, no migration, no selection. Deviations signal evolutionary forces.

canon.name: Allopatric speciation process
canon.form: Geographic barrier → isolated populations → independent divergence via selection/drift → reproductive isolation → new species if secondary contact fails to produce fertile hybrids.

canon.name: Phylogenetic parsimony
canon.form: The preferred phylogeny is the one requiring the fewest evolutionary changes (character state transitions) to explain observed data.

canon.name: Sexual selection
canon.form: A form of natural selection where traits increase mating success rather than survival; intrasexual (competition) and intersexual (mate choice) modes.

glossary.definition: A group of individuals of the same species living in the same area at the same time.
The maximum population size that an environment can sustain indefinitely, given available resources.
Unrestricted population growth at a constant per-capita rate (r), occurring when resources are unlimited (J-shaped curve).
Population growth that slows as density increases due to resource limitation; follows an S-shaped curve, approaching carrying capacity K.
A species that has a disproportionately large effect on community structure relative to its abundance (e.g., sea otters controlling urchin populations).
The ecological role and hypervolume of conditions (food, habitat, timing) used by a species; fundamental niche vs. realized niche.
Two species competing for identical resources cannot coexist indefinitely; one will outcompete and exclude the other.
Position in a food chain defined by the number of energy-transfer steps from primary producers: producers, primary consumers, secondary consumers, etc.
Diagram showing the total dry mass of organisms at each trophic level; typically decreases at higher levels (~10% energy transfer efficiency).
A close, persistent ecological relationship between two species; types include mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism.
Directional change in community composition over time; primary succession starts on bare substrate; secondary succession follows disturbance of existing community.
The rate at which producers fix energy minus their own respiration; the energy available to primary consumers.
A community of organisms (biotic) interacting with their physical environment (abiotic); characterized by energy flow (food chains/webs) and nutrient cycling.

canon.name: Exponential population growth
canon.form: dN/dt = rN; N(t) = N₀·e^(rt)

canon.name: Logistic population growth
canon.form: dN/dt = rN·(K − N)/K; growth rate is maximal at N = K/2 and declines to zero as N approaches K.

canon.name: 10% energy rule
canon.form: Approximately 10% of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next; the rest is lost as heat or used in respiration.

canon.name: Lotka-Volterra predator-prey dynamics
canon.form: Predator and prey populations cycle out of phase: prey increase → predator increase → prey decrease → predator decrease → prey rebound.

canon.name: Nitrogen cycle key processes
canon.form: Nitrogen fixation (N₂ → NH₃ by bacteria) → nitrification (NH₃ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻) → assimilation → ammonification → denitrification (NO₃⁻ → N₂).

canon.name: Community diversity indices
canon.form: Species richness (number of species); species evenness (relative abundance); Shannon diversity index H' = −Σ(pᵢ·ln pᵢ).

subtopics: Central and peripheral nervous system organization

glossary.definition: The electrical potential across the plasma membrane of a resting neuron, typically −70 mV, maintained by K⁺ leak channels and the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase.
A rapid, all-or-none depolarization and repolarization of the neuronal membrane, propagating along the axon without decrement.
Change in membrane potential toward less negative values; occurs when Na⁺ rushes in through voltage-gated Na⁺ channels at threshold (~−55 mV).
A junction between two neurons (or neuron and effector) where electrical or chemical signals are transmitted.
Chemical signal released from a presynaptic terminal that binds to postsynaptic receptors to transmit the nerve signal.
A chemical messenger secreted by an endocrine gland into the bloodstream that regulates physiology and behavior of distant target cells.
Regulatory mechanism in which a change in a variable triggers a response that opposes and reverses that change, maintaining homeostasis.
Excitatory/inhibitory postsynaptic potential; small depolarizations or hyperpolarizations that summate (temporal and spatial summation) to determine if threshold is reached.
Lipid-rich insulation formed by oligodendrocytes (CNS) or Schwann cells (PNS) that speeds action potential propagation by saltatory conduction.
The equilibrium membrane potential for a single ion species, at which the electrical gradient exactly opposes the chemical gradient.
Central neuroendocrine control hub: hypothalamic releasing/inhibiting hormones regulate anterior pituitary hormone secretion, which controls peripheral endocrine glands.
Intracellular signaling molecule (cAMP, IP3, DAG, Ca²⁺) generated in response to hormone-receptor binding, amplifying and transducing the signal.

canon.name: Nernst equation
canon.form: E_ion = (RT/zF) · ln([ion]_out / [ion]_in) ≈ (61.5/z) · log₁₀([ion]_out / [ion]_in) mV at 37°C

canon.name: Action potential sequence
canon.form: Resting (−70 mV) → threshold stimulus → voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open → rapid depolarization (+30 mV) → inactivation of Na⁺ channels → K⁺ channels open → repolarization → hyperpolarization → refractory period → reset.

canon.name: Chemical synapse transmission
canon.form: Action potential arrives → Ca²⁺ influx at presynaptic terminal → vesicle fusion → neurotransmitter released into cleft → binds postsynaptic receptors → EPSP or IPSP → reuptake/degradation.

canon.name: Hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis
canon.form: Low T3/T4 → hypothalamus releases TRH → pituitary releases TSH → thyroid releases T3/T4 → negative feedback to hypothalamus and pituitary.

canon.name: Hormone receptor mechanisms
canon.form: Peptide hormones: bind surface receptors → second messengers (fast). Steroid hormones: lipid-soluble, cross membrane → intracellular receptors → direct gene regulation (slow, sustained).

topics.topic: Physiology: Cardiovascular, Respiratory, and Renal Systems

glossary.definition: Volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute: CO = stroke volume × heart rate; normal ~5 L/min at rest.
Volume of blood ejected by the left ventricle per beat; influenced by preload, contractility, and afterload.
The tetrameric oxygen-transport protein in red blood cells; each subunit carries one heme group that binds one O₂ molecule cooperatively.
Sigmoidal curve describing hemoglobin O₂ saturation as a function of pO₂; rightward shift (Bohr effect) with ↑CO₂, ↑H⁺, ↑temperature, ↑2,3-BPG.
Tiny air sac in the lung across whose thin wall (type I pneumocytes) gas exchange occurs between air and pulmonary capillaries.
Volume of plasma filtered by the kidneys per minute (~125 mL/min); a key indicator of kidney function.
The functional unit of the kidney; consists of the glomerulus, Bowman's capsule, proximal tubule, loop of Henle, distal tubule, and collecting duct.
Recovery of filtered substances (glucose, amino acids, Na⁺, water) from the tubular lumen back into peritubular capillaries.
The intrinsic ability of the heart to increase stroke volume in response to increased venous return (preload), due to greater sarcomere stretch.
Reduced hemoglobin-O₂ affinity at lower pH and higher CO₂, facilitating O₂ unloading in metabolically active tissues.
Hormonal cascade activated by low blood pressure/Na⁺: renin (kidney) → angiotensin I → ACE → angiotensin II → aldosterone release → Na⁺ and water retention → BP increase.
Posterior pituitary peptide that increases water reabsorption in the collecting duct by inserting aquaporin-2 channels, concentrating urine.

canon.name: Cardiac output equation
canon.form: CO = SV × HR; MAP ≈ CO × TPR (total peripheral resistance)

canon.name: Oxygen transport
canon.form: O₂ bound to hemoglobin (97%) and dissolved in plasma (3%); cooperative binding (Hill coefficient ~2.8); O₂ content = (Hb × 1.34 × SaO₂) + (0.003 × pO₂).

canon.name: Urine formation in nephron
canon.form: Glomerular filtration (pressure-driven, ~125 mL/min) → tubular reabsorption (Na⁺, glucose, water) → secretion (H⁺, K⁺, drugs) → final urine ~1.5 L/day.

canon.name: Henderson-Hasselbalch equation (blood pH)
canon.form: pH = pKa + log([HCO₃⁻] / [H₂CO₃]); normal arterial pH 7.4, [HCO₃⁻] 24 mEq/L, PCO₂ 40 mmHg.

canon.name: Ventilation-perfusion matching
canon.form: Efficient gas exchange requires matched V/Q ratios; pulmonary vessels constrict in hypoxic regions (HPV) to divert blood to ventilated alveoli.

glossary.definition: Single-celled organism lacking a membrane-bound nucleus and organelles; bacteria and archaea; smaller and simpler than eukaryotes.
The structural polymer of bacterial cell walls forming a mesh-like layer; thicker in gram-positive, thin outer layer in gram-negative bacteria.
Differential staining technique: gram-positive bacteria (thick peptidoglycan) retain crystal violet (purple); gram-negative (thin PG + outer membrane) stain pink with safranin.
Asexual reproduction of bacteria in which the cell replicates its chromosome, elongates, and divides into two identical daughter cells.
The time required for a bacterial population to double; varies from ~20 minutes (E. coli) to hours or days for slow-growing species.
Viral replication pathway in which the virus hijacks host machinery, replicates, and lyses the host cell to release progeny virions.
Replication pathway in which the viral genome (prophage) integrates into the host chromosome and replicates passively with the host until induced.
Small, circular, extrachromosomal DNA element in bacteria that can replicate autonomously and often carries antibiotic resistance genes.
Direct transfer of DNA (often a plasmid) between two bacteria via a pilus (sex pilus) and cytoplasmic bridge; requires an F-factor.
Bacteria resist antibiotics via: enzymatic inactivation (beta-lactamases), target modification, efflux pumps, or reduced permeability.
Highly resistant, metabolically dormant structure formed by some gram-positive bacteria (Bacillus, Clostridium) to survive extreme conditions.
The protein shell surrounding a viral genome; composed of protomeric subunits (capsomers); icosahedral or helical symmetry.
Prokaryotic microorganisms with no membrane-bound nucleus; diverse metabolic strategies; classified by shape and Gram staining (positive/negative cell wall).

canon.name: Bacterial growth curve
canon.form: Lag phase (metabolic adjustment) → Exponential phase (rapid log growth) → Stationary phase (growth = death, nutrient limitation) → Death phase (exponential die-off).

canon.name: Viral replication cycle
canon.form: Attachment (adsorption) → Penetration (injection/endocytosis) → Uncoating → Biosynthesis (genome replication + protein synthesis) → Assembly → Release (budding or lysis).

canon.name: Horizontal gene transfer in bacteria
canon.form: Three mechanisms: Transformation (uptake of naked DNA), Transduction (bacteriophage-mediated transfer), Conjugation (plasmid transfer via pilus).

canon.name: Antibiotic targets
canon.form: Cell wall synthesis (beta-lactams, vancomycin) → lysis; Protein synthesis 30S (aminoglycosides, tetracyclines) or 50S (macrolides, chloramphenicol); DNA gyrase (fluoroquinolones); RNA polymerase (rifampin).

glossary.definition: Any molecule (typically foreign protein) that is recognized by the immune system and can elicit an adaptive immune response.
Y-shaped glycoprotein produced by plasma cells; binds specific antigens via variable regions (Fab); effector functions via constant region (Fc).
Adaptive immune cell that matures in bone marrow, expresses B-cell receptor (membrane immunoglobulin), and differentiates into antibody-secreting plasma cells or memory B cells.
Adaptive immune cell that matures in the thymus; CD4+ helper T cells coordinate immune responses; CD8+ cytotoxic T cells kill infected cells.
Cell-surface proteins that present peptide antigens to T cells; MHC-I (on all nucleated cells, presents to CD8+ T cells) and MHC-II (on APCs, presents to CD4+ T cells).
Process by which an antigen selectively activates the specific lymphocytes bearing complementary receptors, causing them to proliferate (clonal expansion).
Non-specific, immediate first-line defense that does not require prior exposure; includes physical barriers, phagocytes, complement, and pattern-recognition receptors (TLRs).
Cascade of plasma proteins that opsonize pathogens, promote inflammation, and lyse cells via the membrane attack complex (MAC).
Vascular and cellular response to infection or injury characterized by redness, heat, swelling, and pain; driven by cytokines, histamine, and prostaglandins.
Coating of a pathogen with antibodies or complement (C3b) to enhance recognition and phagocytosis by macrophages and neutrophils.
Small signaling protein secreted by immune cells that regulates immune responses (e.g., interleukins, interferons, TNF-α).
Deliberate exposure to an antigen (attenuated pathogen, subunit, mRNA-encoded protein) to prime immunological memory without causing disease.
A Y-shaped glycoprotein (immunoglobulin) produced by B cells that binds specifically to antigens; the variable regions (Fab) determine specificity.
A protein (on cell surface or intracellular) that binds a specific ligand (hormone, neurotransmitter, drug) and triggers a cellular response via signal transduction.

canon.name: Clonal selection and expansion
canon.form: Antigen binds cognate receptor on naive B or T cell → activation (requires costimulation for T cells) → rapid proliferation → effector cells + memory cells.

canon.name: Antibody structure
canon.form: Two heavy and two light chains connected by disulfide bonds; Fab regions (variable domains VH + VL) determine antigen specificity; Fc region determines isotype function (IgG, IgM, IgA, IgE, IgD).

canon.name: MHC-I vs MHC-II presentation
canon.form: MHC-I: endogenous (cytoplasmic) peptides loaded in ER → present to CD8+ T cells → cytotoxic response (kills infected cells). MHC-II: exogenous (endocytosed) peptides loaded in lysosomes → present to CD4+ T cells → helper response.

canon.name: Primary vs. secondary immune response
canon.form: Primary: slow (7–10 days), low-titer IgM, then IgG; creates memory. Secondary: faster (2–3 days), higher-titer IgG, greater affinity (class switching + somatic hypermutation).

canon.name: Hypersensitivity types
canon.form: Type I: IgE-mediated immediate (allergy, anaphylaxis). Type II: IgG/IgM against cell-surface antigens. Type III: immune complex deposition. Type IV: delayed-type (T cell-mediated, e.g., contact dermatitis, TB skin test).

glossary.definition: Fusion of a sperm and egg to form a diploid zygote, the single-celled precursor to the entire organism.
Rapid mitotic divisions of the zygote without growth, producing the morula and then the blastula/blastocyst.
Morphogenetic process that converts the blastula into a three-layered gastrula (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm) through cell migration.
Three primary cell layers formed during gastrulation: ectoderm (skin, nervous system), mesoderm (muscle, bone, blood), endoderm (gut lining, lungs, liver).
Process by which one group of cells (organizer) signals neighboring cells to change their developmental fate.
Capacity of a cell to develop into any cell type, including extra-embryonic tissues; only the zygote and early blastomeres are totipotent.
Capacity to differentiate into any cell type of the body proper but not extra-embryonic tissues; embryonic stem cells (ESCs) and iPSCs are pluripotent.
A regulatory gene that specifies body segment identity during development; contains a homeobox encoding the DNA-binding homeodomain.
Embryonic precursor to the brain and spinal cord, formed by folding of the neural plate ectoderm; failure to close causes anencephaly or spina bifida.
Programmed cell death involving caspase activation, DNA fragmentation, and cell shrinkage; essential for development (e.g., digit sculpting) and tissue homeostasis.
A diffusible signaling molecule whose concentration gradient provides positional information to cells, specifying different cell fates at different thresholds.

canon.name: Germ layer derivatives
canon.form: Ectoderm → epidermis, nervous system, sensory organs. Mesoderm → muscle, skeleton, circulatory system, kidneys, gonads. Endoderm → digestive and respiratory epithelium, liver, pancreas.

canon.name: Embryonic induction (Spemann organizer)
canon.form: Dorsal lip of the blastopore (Spemann-Mangold organizer) secretes BMP antagonists (noggin, chordin) → permits neural fate in dorsal ectoderm; transplantation of organizer induces a second axis.

canon.name: Hox gene collinearity
canon.form: Hox genes are arranged in chromosomal clusters; gene order on chromosome mirrors expression pattern along anterior-posterior axis (spatial collinearity).

canon.name: Notch-Delta signaling (lateral inhibition)
canon.form: Cell expressing Delta ligand activates Notch on neighbor → cleaves NICD → NICD translocates to nucleus → activates target genes; produces alternating cell fate patterns.

canon.name: Wnt, Hedgehog, and BMP pathways
canon.form: Wnt (via β-catenin), Hedgehog (via Gli TFs), and BMP (via Smad TFs) are conserved developmental signaling pathways used repeatedly for patterning and differentiation.

glossary.definition: Standard reference position: body erect, facing forward, arms at sides with palms facing anteriorly.
Vertical plane dividing the body into left and right portions; midsagittal (median) plane divides into equal halves.
The basic contractile unit of skeletal muscle, from Z-disk to Z-disk, containing overlapping thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments.
Muscle contraction mechanism: myosin heads bind actin, undergo power stroke (pivot), shorten the sarcomere by pulling thin filaments toward center; requires ATP and Ca²⁺.
The dense connective tissue membrane covering the outer surface of bones; contains osteoblasts, osteoclasts, and blood vessels for remodeling.
Freely movable joint with a synovial cavity, articular cartilage, and synovial membrane secreting lubricating fluid; includes hinge, ball-and-socket types.
The deep layer of the skin beneath the epidermis; contains collagen, elastin, blood vessels, sweat glands, hair follicles, and nerve endings.
Finger-like projection of intestinal mucosa that increases surface area for nutrient absorption; epithelium contains enterocytes with microvilli (brush border).
Three protective membranes (dura mater, arachnoid mater, pia mater) surrounding the brain and spinal cord; CSF circulates in the subarachnoid space.
Secondary lymphoid organ along lymphatic vessels that filters lymph, houses B and T lymphocytes, and initiates adaptive immune responses.

canon.name: Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
canon.form: Nerve impulse → ACh released → motor end plate depolarizes → Ca²⁺ released from SR → Ca²⁺ binds troponin → tropomyosin moves → actin sites exposed → myosin cross-bridge cycling → sarcomere shortens.

canon.name: Bone remodeling
canon.form: Osteoclasts (bone resorption) and osteoblasts (bone deposition) continuously remodel bone; regulated by mechanical loading, parathyroid hormone (↑resorption), and calcitonin (↑deposition).

canon.name: Skin layers
canon.form: Epidermis (keratinized stratified squamous; layers from deep to surface: stratum basale → spinosum → granulosum → lucidum → corneum) → dermis → hypodermis.

canon.name: Directional anatomical terms
canon.form: Superior/inferior (above/below), anterior/posterior (front/back), medial/lateral (toward/away from midline), proximal/distal (near/far from origin), superficial/deep.

subtopics: Plant cell structure and cell wall
Plant reproduction and alternation of generations

glossary.definition: Double-membrane organelle in plant cells that conducts photosynthesis; thylakoid membranes house the light reactions; stroma contains the Calvin cycle enzymes.
Process by which plants, algae, and cyanobacteria capture light energy to synthesize glucose from CO₂ and H₂O, releasing O₂.
Flattened membranous sac within the chloroplast; stacked into grana; site of the light-dependent reactions and chlorophyll.
Fluid-filled space of the chloroplast surrounding the thylakoids; site of the Calvin cycle (carbon fixation).
Vascular tissue that conducts water and dissolved minerals upward from roots; composed of dead tracheids and vessel elements; lignified walls.
Vascular tissue that transports photosynthate (sucrose) and other organic solutes bidirectionally; composed of living sieve tube elements and companion cells.
Evaporative loss of water vapor from leaves through stomata; drives the transpiration-cohesion-tension mechanism for water ascent in xylem.
Pores in the leaf epidermis surrounded by guard cells that regulate gas exchange (CO₂ in, O₂ and H₂O out) and transpiration.
Plant hormone (indole-3-acetic acid) promoting cell elongation; responsible for phototropism and gravitropism; produced at shoot apical meristems.
Plant life cycle alternating between diploid sporophyte (spore-producing) and haploid gametophyte (gamete-producing) generations.
Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase; the enzyme that catalyzes carbon fixation in the Calvin cycle; the most abundant protein on Earth.
The wasteful process in which RuBisCO adds O₂ instead of CO₂ to RuBP, consuming energy and releasing CO₂; minimized in C4 and CAM plants.

canon.name: Photosynthesis overall reaction
canon.form: 6 CO₂ + 6 H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6 O₂

canon.name: Light reactions (photophosphorylation)
canon.form: Photosystem II absorbs light → water oxidized → O₂ released → electrons pass through ETC → proton gradient → ATP synthase → ATP; electrons to Photosystem I → NADPH produced.

canon.name: Calvin cycle (dark reactions)
canon.form: Carbon fixation: CO₂ + RuBP (C5) → 2× 3-PGA (via RuBisCO) → Reduction: 3-PGA → G3P (using ATP + NADPH) → Regeneration: G3P → RuBP (net 3 CO₂ → 1 G3P).

canon.name: C3 vs C4 vs CAM photosynthesis
canon.form: C3: CO₂ fixed directly by RuBisCO into 3-PGA; prone to photorespiration. C4: CO₂ pre-fixed as 4-carbon acid in mesophyll, concentrated in bundle sheath for RuBisCO. CAM: stomata open at night, CO₂ stored as malate, released by day; desert adaptation.

canon.name: Cohesion-tension mechanism for water transport
canon.form: Transpiration pull at leaves creates tension → water cohesion (H-bonds) transmits tension down xylem column → water drawn up from roots; adhesion to xylem walls assists.

canon.name: Plant hormone actions
canon.form: Auxin: cell elongation, apical dominance, tropisms. Cytokinin: cell division, delays senescence. Gibberellin: stem elongation, seed germination. Abscisic acid (ABA): stomatal closure, dormancy. Ethylene: fruit ripening, leaf abscission.

glossary.definition: The complete set of chromosomes of a cell, arranged by size and morphology; used to detect chromosomal abnormalities.
Abnormal chromosome number due to nondisjunction; includes trisomy (47 chromosomes) and monosomy (45 chromosomes).
Presence of three copies of chromosome 21; most common viable autosomal trisomy; associated with intellectual disability, characteristic facial features, and heart defects.
A disorder caused by a single mutant allele (heterozygous sufficient); parent-to-child transmission (50% risk); each generation affected (e.g., Huntington's, Marfan syndrome).
A disorder requiring two mutant alleles; carriers are unaffected; 25% recurrence risk to offspring of two carriers (e.g., cystic fibrosis, sickle cell disease, PKU).
Disorder caused by a recessive allele on the X chromosome; primarily affects males (hemizygous); females are carriers; examples: hemophilia A, Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
Failure of homologous chromosomes or sister chromatids to separate during meiosis (or mitosis), producing aneuploid cells.
Epigenetic phenomenon in which gene expression depends on the parent of origin; one allele is silenced by methylation; disruption causes Prader-Willi or Angelman syndrome.
A mutated/overexpressed proto-oncogene that drives uncontrolled cell proliferation; acts dominantly (gain-of-function); examples: RAS, MYC, HER2.
Gene that normally limits cell growth; both alleles must be lost or inactivated to contribute to cancer (two-hit hypothesis); examples: TP53, RB1, BRCA1.
Logarithm of odds score used in genetic linkage analysis; LOD > 3 considered significant evidence for linkage (1000:1 odds).
The proportion of individuals carrying a disease-causing allele who actually manifest the associated phenotype; incomplete penetrance complicates pedigree analysis.

canon.name: Autosomal dominant pedigree pattern
canon.form: Trait appears in every generation; ~50% of offspring of an affected parent are affected; male and female equally affected; affected individuals usually heterozygous.

canon.name: Autosomal recessive pedigree pattern
canon.form: Unaffected parents can have affected children (carrier × carrier → 25% affected); skips generations; consanguinity increases risk; equal sex distribution.

canon.name: X-linked recessive pedigree pattern
canon.form: Primarily males affected; affected males from carrier mothers; no father-to-son transmission; daughters of affected males are obligate carriers.

canon.name: Knudson two-hit hypothesis
canon.form: Two mutational events required to inactivate both copies of a tumor suppressor gene; in hereditary cancers, first hit is germline (inherited), second is somatic; in sporadic, both are somatic.

canon.name: Common chromosomal trisomies
canon.form: Trisomy 21 (Down): intellectual disability, +21. Trisomy 18 (Edwards): severe, often lethal, +18. Trisomy 13 (Patau): severe defects, +13. 47,XXY (Klinefelter): infertility, tall stature. 45,X (Turner): short stature, gonadal dysgenesis.

subtopics: Energy balance and body weight regulation

glossary.definition: A nutrient the body cannot synthesize in adequate amounts and must obtain from the diet; includes essential amino acids, fatty acids, vitamins, and minerals.
The nine amino acids (His, Ile, Leu, Lys, Met, Phe, Thr, Trp, Val) that cannot be synthesized by the human body and must be obtained from diet.
A measure of how rapidly a carbohydrate food raises blood glucose relative to pure glucose (reference = 100); high GI foods cause rapid glucose spikes.
The minimum energy expenditure required to maintain basic physiological functions at rest in a thermoneutral environment; the largest component of total energy expenditure.
Organic micronutrient required in small amounts for normal metabolism; classified as fat-soluble (A, D, E, K) or water-soluble (C, B vitamins).

Fat-soluble vitamin/prohormone; activated form (1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol, calcitriol) promotes Ca²⁺ and phosphate absorption from gut; deficiency causes rickets (children) or osteomalacia (adults).
The most common nutritional deficiency worldwide; inadequate iron reduces hemoglobin synthesis; symptoms include fatigue, pallor, microcytic hypochromic red blood cells.
Protein-lipid complexes that transport cholesterol and triglycerides in the bloodstream; classified by density: chylomicrons, VLDL, IDL, LDL, HDL.
The difference between dietary nitrogen intake and excretion; positive balance (growth/pregnancy), negative balance (starvation, illness), zero balance (healthy adult).
Acetoacetate, beta-hydroxybutyrate, and acetone; produced in the liver from acetyl-CoA during prolonged fasting or carbohydrate restriction; alternative fuel for brain and muscle.
Compound (vitamins C, E; beta-carotene; selenium; polyphenols) that neutralizes reactive oxygen species, reducing oxidative damage to cellular components.

canon.name: Macronutrient energy densities
canon.form: Carbohydrates: 4 kcal/g; Protein: 4 kcal/g; Fat: 9 kcal/g; Alcohol: 7 kcal/g.

canon.name: Digestion and absorption overview
canon.form: Mouth (salivary amylase, mechanical) → stomach (pepsin, acid, protein denaturation) → small intestine (pancreatic enzymes, bile salts, brush-border enzymes) → major absorption in jejunum → large intestine (water, electrolyte reabsorption, fermentation).

canon.name: Fat-soluble vitamins summary
canon.form: Vitamin A: vision, epithelium. Vitamin D: Ca²⁺ homeostasis, bone. Vitamin E: antioxidant, membrane. Vitamin K: coagulation (factors II, VII, IX, X). Deficiency risk with fat malabsorption.

canon.name: B-vitamin coenzyme roles
canon.form: B1 (thiamine): TPP in TCA/glycolysis. B2 (riboflavin): FAD/FMN. B3 (niacin): NAD+/NADP+. B5 (pantothenic acid): CoA. B6 (pyridoxine): PLP in transamination. B9 (folate): 1-C transfers, DNA synthesis. B12 (cobalamin): methylation, nerve function.

canon.name: Insulin and glucagon in nutrient homeostasis
canon.form: Fed state: glucose ↑ → insulin ↑ → promotes glucose uptake, glycogenesis, lipogenesis, protein synthesis. Fasted state: glucose ↓ → glucagon ↑ → promotes glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis, lipolysis.
